children.text: The idea of the 'nice guy' is a long-standing trope in both media and in real-life, and can have very real consequences. Hegemonic masculinity, while being the more 'overt' form of toxic masculinities, is only defined in relation to what it is not, and there exist persistent systems in place to maintain it. One of which is the 'nice guy' or complicit form of masculinity -- not overtly toxic or playing into the role of what is expected from hegemonic masculinity, sometimes because they are barred from it, but nevertheless contributing to the power of it and benefiting from how it shapes society. An example may be seen in shows like the Big Bang Theory, as shown in this video, or in real-life as critically discussed by scholars like Anna Anthropy, who explores how the idea of a nerdy group of guys who banded together to create games in a world that rejected them has persisted in the videogame industry. Ironically ignoring the many women, people of colour, and queer people who were actually rejected and marginalized from the field but still persisted in contributing the industry anyways. These men, who may be rejected from one kind of masculinity (the hegemonic idea) are still benefiting from a white heteropatriarchal system.
